feedback.text: Hello, I want to support the Digital Fairness Act, contention points 1 to 5 are entirely legitimate: 1- I have encountered such practices several times, above all about requests made to my grandmother, clearly targeted to lose her and make her accept unfair conditions. 2- many games use aggressive designs in extreme ways to push for consumption, including the famous Lootbox (which have been confirmed as reinforcing addictive gambling behaviour) and the FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out) concept, which consists of limiting the existence of content over time without the possibility of subsequent acquisition to drive consumption for fear of missing something. 3- the collection of information and the theft of data under the cover of ‘information gathering’ and then targeting ‘attractive’ advertisements on the consumer are well-known and documented topics for a long time. It should also be noted that many services do NOT work or slow down the service if the user refuses to share data. 4- online management of subscriptions is deliberately obstructed and confused by design in many cases, combined with practices aimed at increasing and stacking subscriptions for a long time. 5- influencers’ practices are difficult to judge, on the other hand the abundance of advertising, which is increasingly long and sometimes even hidden and unannounced, is becoming increasingly present. From a general point of view, all these points are fair and call for action to bring equality and justice into a system which is currently operating in a particularly toxic legal uncertainty. More recently, it can also be noted that commercial practices have emerged in the wake of these problems. First, the destruction of content marketed online and in physical format whose systems cease to operate without any support foreseen for this future. I would give an example of the Darkspore game, which I have had evidence of it, but which has disappeared for several years from my game library on the platform owned by its Editor, Electronic Arts, following the closure of the game servers, which made it completely inaccessible for a long time before the removal of a game acquired by the owners’ libraries. Second, large-scale censorship recently taken online as a result of the unilateral decision by a few people putting pressure on Visa e Master Cards’ services that certain content marketed online should be removed because “not aligned with their practices”. Hundreds of content was removed, sometimes even directly from proprietary libraries as a result of this ‘decision’, even though that content was acquired legally. Those two additional points seem to me to be important because, in my view, in connection with the concept of the Digital Fairness Act, it is not fair for the decision of a third party to lead to the loss of legally acquired content, since the content itself is legal in nature. Thank you for your time.
